interest: Teachers and assessment administrators who would be blamed for reform failure, backed by every stakeholder that needs the new instrument to actually work rather than merely be labelled 'coaching-resistant'.
value: Procedural integrity and professional autonomy (validated, co-designed tools before rollout) in tension with reform urgency and top-down administrative control.
fear: That an unvalidated instrument will be imposed, fail its predictive-validity promise, and have its failure attributed to the teachers who administer it rather than the designers who mandated it.
affected: pupils aged 9-10 subject to the new assessment
low-SES and Roma applicants relying on the reform's fairness claim
employers relying on a stable talent signal
assumption: That a genuinely less-coachable assessment with maintained predictive validity can in fact be psychometrically developed for the Hungarian context, and that teacher co-design plus validation are achievable preconditions rather than indefinite blockers.
empirical_uncertainty: Unknown/contested: no validated Hungarian instrument exists, the causal claim that format-change resists coaching rests on moderate evidence (Kertesi & Kezdi 2013, largely a selection-effect finding), so whether such an instrument can be built and validated is unsettled.
